meta.title: Vue.js
meta.description: Review checklist for Vue.js applications (Vue 3, Composition API, Nuxt)

items.text: Does the PR have a clear description of what it does and why?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The PR description should explain the purpose of the change and any relevant context.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Reviewers without context waste time reverse-engineering intent from code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read the PR description. Can you understand what changed and why without reading the code?
baseContent.exampleComment: Could you add more context about why this component was restructured?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: A good PR description saves reviewer time.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each PR should do one thing. Mixing a feature with a refactor makes review harder.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large multi-concern PRs are slow to review and difficult to revert.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the PR touches multiple unrelated areas. Could any part ship independently?
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR adds a new page and also refactors the store. Could we split these?

items.text: Are new components using the Composition API (setup or script setup)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Vue 3's Composition API (especially <script setup>) is the recommended approach for new components, offering better TypeScript support and logic reuse.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The Options API mixes concerns by lifecycle hook. Composition API groups related logic together and enables better composable extraction.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new .vue files for <script setup> or setup() function. Flag new Options API components in a Composition API codebase.
baseContent.exampleComment: This new component uses the Options API. Since the rest of the codebase uses Composition API, could we convert it for consistency?

codeExamples.label: Options API
codeExamples.code:
<script>
export default {
  data() { return { count: 0 } },
  methods: { increment() { this.count++ } }
}
</script>

codeExamples.label: Composition API
codeExamples.code:
<script setup lang="ts">
import { ref } from 'vue'
const count = ref(0)
function increment() { count.value++ }
</script>

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use <script setup> for new components. It is more concise and has better TypeScript inference.

items.text: Are component props properly typed with defaults?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Props should use defineProps with TypeScript types or validator functions, and include sensible defaults.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Untyped props lead to runtime errors and make components hard to use correctly. TypeScript catches misuse at build time.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check defineProps for type annotations. Look for props used without type or default.
baseContent.exampleComment: These props are untyped. Using defineProps<{ title: string; count?: number }>() would catch misuse at compile time.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
<script setup lang="ts">
interface Props {
  title: string
  count?: number
}
const props = withDefaults(defineProps<Props>(), {
  count: 0,
})
</script>

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always type your props. Use withDefaults for optional props with defaults.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Components should declare their emits using defineEmits with TypeScript payload types.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Undeclared emits are not type-checked and can silently fail. Declaring them documents the component's event contract.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for $emit calls without corresponding defineEmits declarations.
baseContent.exampleComment: This component emits 'update' and 'delete' but they are not declared in defineEmits. Adding them enables type checking.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Declare all emits with defineEmits for type safety and documentation.

items.text: Is the component small enough to understand at a glance?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Components should have a single responsibility. If a .vue file exceeds 200-300 lines, it likely needs to be split.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large components are hard to review, test, and maintain. Extracting composables and child components improves reusability.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the line count and number of responsibilities. Can any logic be extracted into a composable?
baseContent.exampleComment: This component is 400 lines and handles form validation, API calls, and rendering. Consider extracting the form logic into a useFormValidation composable.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep components focused. Extract logic into composables, extract UI into child components.

items.text: Is v-model used correctly for two-way binding?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Custom components should support v-model by accepting a modelValue prop and emitting update:modelValue, not by mutating props directly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mutating props breaks Vue's one-way data flow and causes hard-to-debug reactivity issues.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for direct prop mutation. Verify v-model components emit update:modelValue.
baseContent.exampleComment: This component mutates the prop directly instead of emitting. Use defineModel() or emit update:modelValue.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never mutate props. Use defineModel() or emit update:modelValue for two-way binding.

items.text: Are ref() and reactive() used appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: ref() wraps primitives and objects (accessed via .value). reactive() wraps objects (accessed directly). Mixing them inconsistently adds confusion.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Destructuring a reactive() object loses reactivity. Forgetting .value on a ref in script causes silent bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for destructured reactive objects. Verify consistent usage of ref vs reactive within the same codebase.
baseContent.exampleComment: This destructures a reactive object, breaking reactivity. Use toRefs() or switch to ref() for individual values.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const state = reactive({ count: 0, name: '' })
const { count } = state // loses reactivity!

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer ref() for consistency. If using reactive(), never destructure without toRefs().

items.text: Is computed() used for derived state instead of watchers?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Derived values should use computed() rather than watch() + manual update. Computed properties are cached and declarative.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using watch to sync derived state is imperative and error-prone. Computed properties automatically stay in sync.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for watch() that sets a ref based on other reactive values. This is usually a computed in disguise.
baseContent.exampleComment: This watcher just derives fullName from firstName and lastName. A computed property would be simpler and cached.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If you are watching state just to derive other state, use computed() instead.

items.text: Do watchers clean up side effects?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Watch callbacks that create side effects (timers, subscriptions, event listeners) should use the onCleanup parameter to clean up.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without cleanup, watchers can leak timers, create duplicate subscriptions, or fire stale callbacks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for watch() or watchEffect() that set up intervals, event listeners, or subscriptions without cleanup.
baseContent.exampleComment: This watcher sets up a timer but never clears it. Use onCleanup to clear the interval when the watched value changes.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always clean up side effects in watchers using onCleanup.

items.text: Is global state managed through a store (Pinia) rather than provide/inject for complex state?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Complex shared state should use Pinia stores. provide/inject is for dependency injection, not global state management.
baseContent.whyItMatters: provide/inject state is not devtools-friendly, hard to test, and has no built-in patterns for actions or getters.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for provide/inject being used to share complex mutable state across many components.
baseContent.exampleComment: This provide/inject pattern is managing global state with multiple mutations. Consider using a Pinia store for devtools support and testability.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use Pinia for global state. Use provide/inject for dependency injection and simple config.

items.text: Do v-for loops have unique :key attributes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every v-for loop must have a unique, stable :key. Using the array index as key causes bugs when items are reordered or removed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without proper keys, Vue cannot efficiently diff the DOM and may reuse the wrong component instances, causing stale state.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check all v-for directives for :key. Verify the key is a unique identifier, not the loop index.
baseContent.exampleComment: This v-for uses the index as :key. If items can be reordered or deleted, use item.id instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always use a unique, stable identifier as :key in v-for. Never use index for dynamic lists.

items.text: Are v-if and v-for not used on the same element?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Using v-if and v-for on the same element is ambiguous and has changed behavior between Vue 2 and Vue 3.
baseContent.whyItMatters: In Vue 3, v-if has higher priority than v-for, which means the loop variable is not accessible in the condition. This causes runtime errors.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for elements with both v-if and v-for directives.
baseContent.exampleComment: This element has both v-if and v-for. Wrap the v-for in a <template> and put v-if on the child element.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never put v-if and v-for on the same element. Use <template v-for> with v-if on the child.

items.text: Are component styles scoped to prevent leaking?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Component <style> blocks should use scoped or CSS Modules to prevent styles from affecting other components.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unscoped styles leak globally and cause unpredictable styling conflicts that are hard to debug.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check <style> tags for the scoped attribute or module attribute.
baseContent.exampleComment: This component's styles are not scoped. Add <style scoped> to prevent them from leaking to other components.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use <style scoped> or CSS Modules. Only use global styles intentionally in a dedicated file.

items.text: Is complex logic kept out of templates?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Templates should contain simple expressions. Complex logic should be in computed properties or methods.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Complex template expressions are hard to read, cannot be reused, and are not cached.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for multi-line expressions, chained method calls, or complex ternaries in template bindings.
baseContent.exampleComment: This template expression is doing filtering and mapping inline. Extract it into a computed property for readability.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep templates simple. Move logic into computed properties or methods.

items.text: Are heavy components lazy-loaded where appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Large components not needed on initial render should use defineAsyncComponent() or dynamic import() for code splitting.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Bundling everything upfront increases initial load time. Lazy loading defers non-critical code until it is needed.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for large components (modals, charts, editors) that are imported statically but only shown conditionally.
baseContent.exampleComment: This chart component is 50KB but only shown when the user clicks a tab. Consider lazy loading it with defineAsyncComponent.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Lazy-load components that are not visible on initial render.

items.text: Are event listeners and subscriptions cleaned up in onUnmounted?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Global event listeners, WebSocket connections, and manual DOM subscriptions must be removed when the component unmounts.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Leaked listeners accumulate over time, causing memory leaks and phantom behavior from unmounted components.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for addEventListener, setInterval, or external library subscriptions without corresponding cleanup in onUnmounted.
baseContent.exampleComment: This component adds a resize listener in onMounted but never removes it. Add a cleanup in onUnmounted.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every subscription created in onMounted needs a corresponding teardown in onUnmounted.

baseContent.whatItMeans: Avoid reactive dependencies that change frequently but do not affect the rendered output. Use shallowRef for large objects that replace rather than mutate.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unnecessary re-renders degrade performance, especially in lists or complex component trees.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for reactive objects that are replaced frequently. Look for computed properties with expensive calculations that could be cached.
baseContent.exampleComment: This ref holds a large array that is replaced on every keystroke. Consider debouncing the update or using shallowRef.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Be mindful of reactivity granularity. Use shallowRef and shallowReactive for large data structures.
